title: Product Emergency Process — Stakeholder Edition
description: Moonfare's official Product Emergency Process for stakeholders. Learn to recognize, classify, and respond to emergencies — covering the severity matrix, response roles, communication channels, and the full incident lifecycle. Built with real source material from the Moonfare Emergency Playbook.
author: Hasan Avsar — Product Ops

landingPage.tagline: When seconds matter — know your role.

modules.title: Module 1: Introduction & Why It Matters
modules.description: Foundations: what counts as a Product Emergency at Moonfare and why your role as a stakeholder matters from minute one.

slides.title: Product Emergency Process

content.content: /moonfare-logo-white.png
When something breaks at Moonfare, the response is fast, structured, and transparent. This course will teach you what to expect — and how you fit in.

slides.coverSubtitle: A Stakeholder's Guide to Crisis Response at Moonfare

content.content: Learning Objectives
By the end of this 25-minute course, you will be confident participating in any Product Emergency at Moonfare — whether you are reporting one, receiving updates, or relaying information to partners.
Stakeholders, Heads of Department, Partnerships, Investor Relations, Compliance, and anyone who receives #emergency_stakeholders notifications.

learningObjectives.text: Recognize what qualifies as a Product Emergency at Moonfare
Read and apply the 5-level severity matrix
Identify who owns what during an active emergency
Know which Slack channels and updates to follow
Act correctly when an emergency notification reaches you

content.content: Defining the Terms
Not every bug is an emergency. Moonfare uses strict, measurable thresholds to escalate.

accordionItems.title: Incident
accordionItems.content: An unexpected behavior of a tool or flow that differs from its normal function. Most incidents are not emergencies.

accordionItems.title: Emergency
accordionItems.content: A specific, severe class of incident where Moonfare's existence, revenue, or reputation is at meaningful risk and the entire Product + Tech organization may need to drop everything.

accordionItems.title: Post-mortem
accordionItems.content: A blameless review held after every emergency to capture what happened, what we learned, and how to prevent recurrence. All documented in Confluence.

content.content: If you are unsure whether an issue is an emergency, do not guess. Contact the Product Ops team — they own the classification call.

slides.title: Why Stakeholders Are Central to the Process

data.layoutText:
You are not a passive observer

During an emergency, the Liaison/Incident Manager actively pulls stakeholders into the loop because:

  • Partners often hear about issues before internal teams do
  • You may need to update Quintet, B2B partners, or major investors
  • Your visibility prevents miscommunication and reputational damage
  • Compliance and legal sign-offs may depend on your team

content.content: Quick Check

options.text: Any bug reported by a customer
An unexpected behaviour that affects a small number of cosmetic UI elements
A severe issue where Moonfare's existence, revenue, or reputation is at meaningful risk
Any Jira ticket marked as a bug

questions.explanation: Correct. The Emergency classification is reserved for severe, measurable business impact — not every bug. The Emergency Playbook is explicit on this.
questions.text: Which statement best describes a Product Emergency at Moonfare?

questions.explanation: False. The Emergency Playbook says the opposite — when unsure, contact Product Ops. They own the classification call. Over-escalating creates fatigue and dilutes future responses.
questions.text: True or False: If you are unsure whether something is an emergency, you should escalate it as one to be safe.

modules.title: Module 2: The Severity Matrix
modules.description: How Moonfare classifies emergencies — the 5 tiers, SLAs, and how to recognize each.

content.content: The matrix exists so anyone — engineer, PM, partner — can classify an issue the same way. This module walks you through each level.
Click each tab to compare severity tiers

tabItems.title: 🚨 EMERGENCY
tabItems.content:
SLA: Response <20 min · Resolution <4 hours

Trigger: Moonfare's existence is at risk. The entire Product and Tech organization is alarmed.

  • Production, investor-facing app only (app.moonfare.com)
  • Core functionality not working — investors cannot invest
  • 100% of active users affected
  • Potential financial impact > €100,000
  • High expected reputational damage to Moonfare or partner brand

tabItems.title: 🔴 HIGHEST
tabItems.content:
SLA: Response ~20 min · Resolution 1–72 hours

Trigger: Core functionality affecting internal stakeholders. Revenue impacted.

  • Production issue that is not full emergency
  • e.g., 1 fund not showing, password reset broken, internal tool down (AP, Demo, Staging0)
  • 30–99% of active users affected
  • Potential financial impact ≥ €10,000

tabItems.title: 🟠 HIGH
tabItems.content:
SLA: Response within 1 working day · Resolution within a sprint

Trigger: Core functionality issues, but a workaround exists.

  • e.g., investor cannot log in, tax report cannot be downloaded
  • 10–30% of active users affected
  • Requires urgent attention but is not existential

tabItems.title: 🟡 MEDIUM
tabItems.content:
SLA: Response within 1 working day · Resolution within 3+ sprints

Trigger: Cosmetic or adjacent functionality issues.

  • e.g., a recurring error message despite functionality working
  • Data displayed with non-critical errors
  • UI issues

tabItems.title: 🟢 LOW
tabItems.content:
SLA: Response within 1 working day · Resolution within 3+ sprints

Trigger: Minor cosmetic issues with no functional impact.

  • A button moved to a different position by accident
  • Hard-coded copy contains a spelling mistake
  • Colours displayed wrong

content.content: Response & Resolution Targets
The single most important reference table in this course.
If you do not see an update on a Severity-1 (Emergency) within 30 minutes, something is wrong with the process — not just the bug. Escalate to Product Ops.
Tap each card to reveal the correct severity
These are real categories taken from the Moonfare Emergency Playbook.

flipCards.front: A data breach exposing investor PII
flipCards.back: 🚨 EMERGENCY — Compliance ticket required, Security team paged immediately.
🟡 Not an emergency — handled by Security as standard incident.

flipCards.front: Functional issue affecting all investors at fund close
flipCards.back: 🚨 EMERGENCY — revenue at stake, full Product + Tech mobilized.

flipCards.front: An investor has requested GDPR deletion
flipCards.back: 🟠 HIGH — must be actioned within a working day.

flipCards.front: A button has moved by accident in the UI
flipCards.back: 🟢 LOW — handled in normal sprint cadence.
🚨 EMERGENCY — Security L2 paged, Compliance ticket opened.

content.content: Apply the Matrix

options.text: Tax report download fails for one investor

questions.explanation: Correct! Severity is determined by user impact, financial exposure, and reputational risk — in that order. A data breach is automatically Emergency tier; cosmetic issues sit at Low.
questions.text: Drag the following scenarios from MOST severe (top) to LEAST severe (bottom) according to Moonfare's matrix:

content.content: Drag each scenario into the correct severity bucket
This is exactly the kind of triage Product Ops does dozens of times per week.

cardSortCards.text: Tax document download error for 1 user

content.content: Test Your Calibration

options.text: A spelling mistake in a marketing email
A data breach exposing investor information
One investor unable to log in
A misaligned button on the dashboard

questions.explanation: A data breach is one of the named EMERGENCY categories in the Playbook, alongside total platform outages and critical security vulnerabilities.
questions.text: Which of the following automatically qualifies as an EMERGENCY?

questions.explanation: Correct. The HIGH tier covers 10–30% of users impacted on critical functions. EMERGENCY requires 100%; HIGHEST is 30–99%.
questions.text: If 25% of active users cannot complete a critical action, this is most likely:

questions.explanation: True. That is the explicit trigger language in the Emergency Playbook — Moonfare's existence is at risk.
questions.text: EMERGENCY tier requires the entire Product and Tech organization to drop their work and respond.

modules.title: Module 3: Roles & Responsibilities
modules.description: Who owns what during an active emergency — and where stakeholders fit (and don't fit).

slides.title: Roles & Responsibilities

content.content: Five distinct roles activate during a Moonfare emergency. Knowing them prevents duplicated effort and missed handoffs.

slides.coverSubtitle: Who does what when the alert fires

content.content: Click to expand each role

accordionItems.title: 🎯 L1 — Liaison / Incident Manager (Prod Ops)
accordionItems.content:
Owners: Lily + Hasan + rotational PMs

  • Quickly identify incident status
  • Pull in appropriate departments
  • Provide internal & external updates
  • Decide communication channels
  • Send Emergency Update email to Stakeholders
  • Create Compliance ticket and link to Product ticket
  • Manage flow from creation to post-mortem

accordionItems.title: 🛠️ L2 — Engineering Subject Matter Experts
accordionItems.content:
Owners: EMs per domain during work hours; 1 on-call engineer outside

  • Establish next steps for resolution
  • Investigate and assess impact
  • Execute fixes
  • Support post-mortem

accordionItems.title: 🧠 L3 — Senior SMEs (Engineering)
accordionItems.content:
Owner: Elena

  • Assess severity together with Prod Ops
  • Brought in when escalated by engineers
  • Confirms action points and fixes
  • Attends post-mortem (optional)

accordionItems.title: 🔒 Security Team (L1/L2)
accordionItems.content:
Lead: Hassan

  • Quickly identifies if Security needs to be involved
  • Initiates security assessment
  • Fills compliance incident form
  • Updates and follows through on the security ticket

accordionItems.title: 👑 Command Staff (Leadership)
accordionItems.content:
Members: Andrew · Elena · Hassan

  • Visibility on every emergency
  • Make major business decisions (e.g., partner comms, public statement)
  • All information flows up to them

data.layoutText:
The Stakeholder Role

Stakeholders are not in the operational response chain — but you are critical to:

  • Receiving status via #emergency_stakeholders
  • Relaying information to partners (Quintet, B2B) — coordinated by Max & Nicolai
  • Coordinating with Investor Relations, Compliance, Legal
  • NOT joining the active war-room unless invited

Discipline matters: jumping into the response uninvited slows it down.

content.content: Role Recall

matchingPairs.left: Sends Emergency Update email to Stakeholders
Investigates and executes the technical fix
Assesses severity together with Prod Ops on escalation
Creates Compliance Incident Form for data breaches
Makes major business decisions during the incident

questions.explanation: Each role has clear, non-overlapping ownership. The L1 / Prod Ops Liaison is the spine of the entire process.
questions.text: Match each responsibility to the role that owns it:

content.content: Do these. Don't do the rest.

checklistItems.title: Acknowledge the #emergency_stakeholders Slack ping
checklistItems.description: A simple ✅ or 'received' reaction is enough — confirms the loop is closed.

checklistItems.title: Read the linked Jira ticket BEFORE asking questions
checklistItems.description: All updates are posted there every 30 minutes. Most questions are already answered.

checklistItems.title: Hold partner communication until cleared by Prod Ops
checklistItems.description: Premature messaging to Quintet or B2B partners can amplify reputational damage.

checklistItems.title: Forward stakeholder concerns through the L1 Liaison
checklistItems.description: Do not approach engineering directly — it pulls them off the fix.

checklistItems.title: Attend the post-mortem if invited
checklistItems.description: Stakeholder perspective is critical for capturing reputational and partnership impact.

modules.title: Module 4: Communication & Updates
modules.description: Channels, cadence, and the standard templates Moonfare uses to maintain transparency under pressure.

slides.title: Communication & Updates

content.content: Clear, consistent communication is what separates a controlled emergency from a panic. Here is the exact infrastructure Moonfare uses.

slides.coverSubtitle: The channels, the cadence, the templates

content.content: Where information flows
Each channel has a precise audience and purpose. Sending the wrong message to the wrong channel is itself a process violation.

tableRows: Heads of Dept, Product, Eng, Prod Ops
All Eng, Product, Prod Ops, Security

content.content: #emergency_stakeholders is the one to watch. Everything else is operational noise that will distract from your job.
Every 30 minutes — non-negotiable

timelineEvents.title: Acknowledgement
timelineEvents.description: L1 Liaison comments: 'Product/Engineering is aware of the emergency and investigating.'

timelineEvents.title: First Investigation Update
timelineEvents.description: First Jira comment with initial findings; #emergency_stakeholders pinged.

timelineEvents.title: Status Updates
timelineEvents.description: Jira ticket gets a fresh comment. Even 'no change' is communicated explicitly.

timelineEvents.title: Resolution Comment
timelineEvents.description: Each action item marked Resolved with POC. Ticket closed, follow-ups linked.

timelineEvents.title: Stakeholder Email
timelineEvents.description: Moderate emergency: 1 summary email. Severe: hourly emails throughout.

content.content: The exact language used at each stage
Consistency is intentional. The same words show up every time so receivers learn to read them at a glance.

tabItems.title: 🟥 Triggered
tabItems.content: "Product has acknowledged this request. We will come back soon on next steps."

tabItems.title: 🔍 Investigating
tabItems.content: "Product/Engineering is aware of the emergency and investigating this. Please follow Jira ticket for all updates (every 30 mins)."

tabItems.title: 🟨 Identified
tabItems.content:
"Product/Engineering have identified the cause of this emergency. XYZ resulted in XYZ.
They have outlined the following action points to resolve this:
• XYZ : POC
• XYZ : POC
• XYZ : POC
We will update you in 30 mins."

tabItems.title: 🟦 Monitoring
tabItems.content: "There are no new updates regarding the action points. We will update when there is, within the next 30 mins."

tabItems.title: 🟩 Resolved
tabItems.content:
"Update:
• XYZ : POC – Resolved
• XYZ : POC – Resolved
We will go ahead and close this ticket. For further investigation please follow linked ticket."

content.content: Channels & Cadence

questions.explanation: Correct. #emergency_stakeholders exists specifically so stakeholders get the right level of detail without being overwhelmed by operational chatter.
questions.text: As a stakeholder, which Slack channel is your PRIMARY source of truth?

options.text: Every 30 minutes — even if there's no change

questions.explanation: Every 30 minutes is the explicit cadence. 'No new updates' is itself a valid update — silence is not allowed.
questions.text: How often must Jira ticket updates be posted during an active emergency?

questions.explanation: Correct. Severe emergencies = email every hour. Moderate emergencies = 1 summary email within 2 hours of resolution.
questions.text: Severe emergencies trigger a stakeholder email every _____.

modules.title: Module 5: The Lifecycle of a Response
modules.description: Detect · Respond · Identify · Resolve · Improve — the five values, walked through with a realistic Friday-evening scenario.

content.content: Five values frame the entire process. Internalize these and you will always know what stage you are in.
These are the principles, not just steps

timelineEvents.title: Detect
timelineEvents.description: We should know there is a problem (via monitoring) BEFORE stakeholders do. Synthetic monitoring + PagerDuty + alerts in #mf-oncall.

timelineEvents.title: Respond
timelineEvents.description: Always acknowledge an emergency — let stakeholders know where to find updates. The 'response' SLA stops the moment the ticket is acknowledged.

timelineEvents.title: Identify
timelineEvents.description: Identify who needs to be involved and what the next steps are. L1 pulls in L2 / L3 / Security / Infra as needed.

timelineEvents.title: Resolve
timelineEvents.description: Resolve as fast as possible. Action points are tracked publicly with a POC against each one.

timelineEvents.title: Improve
timelineEvents.description: Always review and learn. Every emergency triggers a post-mortem in Confluence — blameless, and shared with Command Staff.

data.layoutText:
Two ways an emergency is detected

1. Automated — synthetic monitoring + New Relic + PagerDuty fire automatically. The Liaison sees it before any human reports it. This is the goal.

2. Reported — an employee or partner files a ticket via the helpdesk. Prod Ops then validates whether it qualifies as an emergency.

If you, as a stakeholder, see an issue before the alert fires — that itself is signal. Report it to Product Ops with timestamps and screenshots.

content.content: Make the right call
You are a stakeholder. You receive a partner WhatsApp message at 18:30 on a Friday: "Three of my investors say they cannot complete their KYC." Walk through the decision tree.

data.scenarioDescription: A scenario that tests every key principle from this course.

scenarioSteps.text: It's 18:30 Friday. A partner reports 3 investors cannot complete KYC. Your first move?

choices.text: Open a Jira ticket via the helpdesk and tag Product Ops + on-call
choices.feedback: Correct first move — formal capture before anything else.

choices.text: Message the engineering EM directly on Slack
choices.feedback: Skipping the helpdesk breaks the audit trail and SLA tracking.

choices.text: Wait until Monday to gather more data
choices.feedback: Delay on a potential KYC blocker is unacceptable.

scenarioSteps.text: The EM helps, but no Jira ticket exists. Two hours later more reports come in. What now?

choices.text: Open the ticket retroactively and document everything
choices.feedback: Recovered — but you lost the response-SLA timer. Always start with the ticket.

scenarioSteps.text: By Monday morning 40+ investors are blocked and Quintet calls Andrew directly.
scenarioSteps.endMessage: ❌ This is exactly the scenario the Emergency Process exists to prevent. Stakeholders are critical to early detection — never sit on partner reports.

scenarioSteps.text: Ticket opened. Prod Ops L1 acknowledges within 15 min. Partner asks you for an ETA. Your reply?

choices.text: "Engineering is investigating. I'll share confirmed updates as they come — please don't act on rumors."
choices.feedback: Excellent — you set expectations without overcommitting.

choices.text: "Should be fixed in an hour, don't worry."
choices.feedback: Never commit on behalf of engineering — that's a reputational time-bomb.

choices.text: Forward the partner the link to the live #emergency_product channel
choices.feedback: Stakeholders never share internal operational channels with partners.

scenarioSteps.text: An hour passes; the issue isn't fixed. The partner is angrier than before.
scenarioSteps.endMessage: ❌ Over-promising during an emergency is one of the most damaging stakeholder mistakes. Always defer to the Liaison's confirmed timing.

scenarioSteps.text: Internal Slack messages are now being screenshotted by an external party.
scenarioSteps.endMessage: ❌ #emergency_product is internal-only. Stakeholder-to-partner comms must be sanitized and time-delayed.

scenarioSteps.text: Ticket is in the Identified stage. L1 says it'll be 90 minutes to resolution. What's your next stakeholder action?

choices.text: Notify Max + Nicolai per process so they can pre-brief Quintet
choices.feedback: Perfect — partner comms route via the partnerships team, not directly.

choices.text: Personally call the affected investors to apologize
choices.feedback: Investor outreach during an active incident is reserved for IR — coordinated, not freelanced.

scenarioSteps.text: IR is now mid-call with two of the same investors. Messaging conflicts emerge.
scenarioSteps.endMessage: ❌ Coordination is everything. IR owns investor outreach during incidents.

scenarioSteps.text: The fix ships at 20:15. L1 sends the resolution email at 20:45. You forward an internal-only note to your team and attend the post-mortem on Monday.
scenarioSteps.endMessage: ✅ Textbook stakeholder response. You captured the issue early, deferred to the process, protected partner relationships, and contributed to learning.

content.content: Lifecycle Mastery

questions.explanation: True. The first principle is Detect — monitoring should catch issues before stakeholders do. Stakeholder-first detection is signal that monitoring needs improvement.
questions.text: If a stakeholder spots an issue before monitoring catches it, that is a process failure.

questions.explanation: False. The fifth value — Improve — requires a blameless post-mortem in Confluence after every emergency. Resolution is not the finish line.
questions.text: Once an emergency is resolved, the process is complete.

options.text: Root cause + outlined action points + POCs + 30-min update commitment

questions.explanation: Identified-stage updates explicitly include cause, action points with POCs, and a commitment to update in 30 minutes.
questions.text: During the 'Identified' stage, what should stakeholders expect to see?

modules.title: Module 6: Final Assessment
modules.description: Bring it all together. 10 mixed-format questions covering severity, roles, communication, and lifecycle.

slides.title: Final Assessment

content.content: Bring everything together. You may retry — but each attempt counts.
/moonfare-logo-white.png

slides.coverSubtitle: 10 questions — 80% to pass, certificate awarded on success

content.content: Part 1 of 2

questions.explanation: Cosmetic with no functional impact = Low. Will be addressed in standard sprint cadence.
questions.text: A misaligned button on the investor dashboard is reported. What severity?

options.text: Open Jira + Compliance ticket + page Security + alert Command Staff
Wait for confirmation before paging anyone

questions.explanation: Data breaches are EMERGENCY tier. Compliance ticket is mandatory, Security is paged, Command Staff has visibility.
questions.text: A potential data breach is identified. Which actions MUST happen?

matchingPairs.right: "Product has acknowledged this request. We will come back soon on next steps."
"Product/Engineering is aware of the emergency and investigating this."
"They have outlined the following action points to resolve this emergency."
"We will go ahead and close this ticket."

questions.explanation: Templates are deliberately consistent — readers learn to scan them at a glance.
questions.text: Match each emergency stage with its template language:

options.text: Every 30 minutes — even with no change

questions.explanation: 30 minutes minimum, even if the message is 'no change'.
questions.text: Update cadence on a live emergency Jira ticket:

questions.explanation: False. The dedicated channel is for active responders only. Stakeholders use #emergency_stakeholders.
questions.text: Stakeholders should join the dedicated emergency channel automatically created by PagerDuty.

content.content: Part 2 of 2

options.text: Call the partner directly with what you know
Wait — partner comms route via Max & Nicolai (Partnerships team)
Forward the Jira ticket to the partner
Post in #emergency_stakeholders asking for an ETA

questions.explanation: Max & Nicolai own the partner relay. Direct stakeholder-to-partner messages bypass coordination and risk inconsistent messaging.
questions.text: Your responsibility as a stakeholder when partner-facing comms are needed:

options.text: Respond to the emergency ticket (stops first-response SLA)
Run play to trigger L2 Engineers

questions.explanation: This is the L1 Liaison's actual checklist, in the prescribed order. Acknowledgement always comes first to start the clock.
questions.text: Rank these emergency response activities in the correct chronological order:

questions.explanation: EMERGENCY = > €100,000 potential impact. HIGHEST = ≥ €10,000.
questions.text: EMERGENCY tier financial impact threshold:

questions.explanation: False. All status comes through the L1 Liaison or via Jira/Slack. Direct pinging pulls engineers off the fix.
questions.text: It is acceptable for a stakeholder to ping engineers directly during an emergency for status.

options.text: An apology email to all customers

questions.explanation: Every emergency triggers a blameless post-mortem in Confluence. This is the 5th value: Improve.
questions.text: After resolution, what is required for every emergency?

content.content: You've completed the Product Emergency Process course
You now understand how Moonfare classifies, escalates, communicates, resolves, and learns from emergencies — and exactly where stakeholders fit in that flow.

Review the source documentation when in doubt:
• Emergency Playbook (Confluence, Product space)
• 24/7 Emergency Threshold Decision Framework
• L2 Replatforming Product Runbook
If you scored ≥80%, your certificate has been generated.
